behaviors.description: A plugin whose manifest is missing a required field is rejected before any code from that plugin is executed.
behaviors.justification: Manifest validation is deterministic parsing logic (JSON parse + field presence check). The real failure—executing plugin code despite invalid manifest—is a control-flow bug observable by verifying that require() is never called when validation fails. Mocking file reads and require() tracking captures this without needing real I/O; a mock won't hide the failure (if the loader calls require() despite failed validation, the mock call will be recorded).
behaviors.test_description: Create a manifest with a missing required field (e.g., no 'name' property). Provide it to the loader with mocked file reads. Mock require() and track whether it's called for the invalid plugin. Assert that require() is never invoked and that the loader rejects the plugin synchronously.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Assumes JSON parsing and file read mocking work correctly. Does not test filesystem encoding issues, but the behavior is about control flow (reject before execution), not about file handling.

behaviors.description: When plugin A declares a dependency on plugin B, plugin B's onLoad hook is called before plugin A's.
behaviors.justification: Hook call order is observable at unit level via mocked hook tracking. The topological sort and hook invocation order are pure control-flow logic. Real failure (wrong hook call order) is caught by a mock that records when each hook is called. Mocks will not hide this—if the loader calls hooks in the wrong sequence, the call order in the mock will show it.
behaviors.test_description: Create manifests A (depends on B) and B. Create onLoad hooks for each that record their call timestamp or append to a call-order list. Invoke the loader and assert that B.onLoad is called strictly before A.onLoad.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Tests hook call order specifically. Assumes that onLoad hook order is the correct abstraction for verifying dependency constraints. If real plugin system failures manifest in module loading order (not hook order), integration tests would be necessary.

behaviors.description: When two plugins each declare a dependency on the other, the loader reports the cycle and loads neither.
behaviors.justification: Cycle detection is a pure graph algorithm. Real failure (cycle undetected or both plugins loaded) is observable by providing circular manifests and verifying that neither onLoad hook is called and that an error is reported. Mocked manifests and hooks are sufficient; mocks won't hide the failure.
behaviors.test_description: Create manifests A (depends on B) and B (depends on A). Mock their onLoad hooks. Invoke the loader and assert that: (1) neither hook is called, (2) the loader reports a cycle error (via exception, log, or return value), and (3) the loader terminates cleanly without crashing.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Tests pure graph algorithm without filesystem or module loading. Assumes cycle detection and reporting are implemented as documented.

behaviors.description: A plugin's onLoad hook is only invoked after the plugin's entry module has been imported and executed.
behaviors.justification: The behavior requires verifying that the module is in a 'ready' state—imports resolved, code executed, side effects visible—before onLoad is called. A unit test with mocked require() can only verify call order, not actual module initialization. Mocking always returns a fake module, which hides the real failure: a loader that calls onLoad before the module's require() completes. Integration testing with real plugin modules (with observable side effects or exports) reveals whether the loader respects actual Node.js module loading semantics.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real plugin module that exports a marker object (e.g., { initialized: true }) and performs a side effect (e.g., sets a module-level flag). Configure the loader to load this plugin. Before the loader invokes the plugin's onLoad hook, inspect the module's exports and verify that the side effect has occurred. Assert that the module state is consistent with successful require() execution.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration testing with real modules is necessary to verify module readiness. Does not test filesystem errors; assumes modules load cleanly from a test directory or in-memory source.

behaviors.description: When a plugin throws an error during its onLoad hook, the loader continues initializing the remaining plugins that do not depend on it.
behaviors.justification: Error handling and dependency-aware skipping are control-flow decisions. Real failure (crashing on error, skipping non-dependent plugins incorrectly) is observable by mocking plugin hooks with one hook throwing an exception. Verify that the loader catches the error, does not call onLoad for dependent plugins, calls onLoad for non-dependent plugins, and continues without crashing. Mocks will not hide this; if the loader crashes or makes wrong calls, the test execution will show it.
behaviors.test_description: Create three plugin manifests: A (onLoad throws), B (depends on A), and C (independent). Mock the hooks so A.onLoad raises an exception. Invoke the loader and assert: (1) A.onLoad is called and throws, (2) B.onLoad is never called, (3) C.onLoad is called successfully, and (4) the loader completes without crashing.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Tests control-flow logic (exception handling, dependency skipping). Assumes exception handling is straightforward and does not depend on actual plugin code behavior.
